sections.heading: Division 3—Confidentiality of information
sections.title: Confidentiality of information handling
sections.summary: Protected information obtained for this Act's purposes may only be disclosed for specific authorized purposes. Unauthorized use or disclosure of such information constitutes an offence with civil penalties. Records must be maintained for the purposes of this Act, including significant and notifiable actions.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires strict confidentiality for information related to foreign acquisitions and takeovers, impacting data storage practices for sensitive cross-border information.

requirements.text: Protected information may be disclosed only for certain purposes
A person who obtains, uses or discloses protected information without authorization commits an offence
Records must be made and kept for the purposes of this Act

sections.source_quote: Information that is obtained for the purposes of this Act (called protected information) may be disclosed only for certain purposes.
sections.relevance_reason: Confidentiality, unauthorized disclosure, records retention

sections.heading: Authorisation of disclosures where consent or to person who
sections.title: Authorisation of disclosures with consent
sections.summary: Disclosures of information must be authorized by consent or to specific persons as permitted by this Act. The Act establishes conditions for when disclosures are permissible without consent, including for national security purposes. Unauthorized disclosures constitute offences with civil penalties.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires explicit authorization for disclosures involving sensitive foreign acquisition information, impacting data sharing practices across borders.

requirements.text: Disclosures of information must be authorized by consent or to specific persons

sections.source_quote: Authorisation of disclosures where consent or to person who
sections.relevance_reason: Core data consent and disclosure requirements

sections.heading: Endnotes
sections.title: Regulations for tax administration
sections.summary: This section allows the Governor-General to make regulations for matters required or permitted by the Act, including indexing values or amounts. It permits regulations to incorporate other instruments without modification.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant for data transfer impact assessments as it governs regulatory frameworks for tax data, affecting cross-border data processing.

requirements.text: Governor-General may make regulations prescribing matters required or permitted by Act
Regulations may provide for indexing a value or amount
Regulations may incorporate other instruments without modification

sections.source_quote: The Governor-General may make regulations prescribing matters: (a) required or permitted by this Act to be prescribed by the regulations; or (b) necessary or convenient to be prescribed for carrying out or giving effect to this Act.
sections.relevance_reason: Tax administration, no data compliance relevance

sections.heading: Endnotes (cont.)
sections.relevance_reason: Endnotes are not part of the legal text

sections.heading: Endnotes (cont.)
sections.title: Definitions of key terms for regulatory compliance
sections.summary: This section defines numerous terms used throughout the Act, including 'officer of an entity', 'residential land', 'relevant entity', and 'subsidiary'. It specifies the conditions under which individuals or entities qualify as officers, the criteria for residential land, and the scope of relevant entities that hold Australian assets. The definitions are critical for understanding the regulatory framework and determining who is subject to the Act's provisions.
sections.dtia_summary: These definitions establish the scope of entities and individuals subject to the Act, which is essential for determining whether data transfers or processing activities fall under the regulatory framework. They help identify who qualifies as an 'officer' or 'relevant entity' for compliance purposes.

requirements.text: officer of an entity includes the following: (a) for a corporation—a director of the corporation; (b) for a unit trust: (i) the trustee of which is an individual—the trustee; (ii) the trustee of which is a corporation—a director of the trustee; (iii) in any case—any other individual involved in the central management and control of the trust; (c) a person who is, or a person in a group of persons who are, in a position to determine the investments or policy of the entity or a trustee of the entity; (d) a person who makes, or participates in making, decisions that affect the whole, or a substantial part of, the business of the entity; (e) a person who has the capacity to affect significantly the financial standing of the entity; (f) a receiver and manager of any part of the business of the entity appointed under a power contained in any instrument; (g) an administrator of the entity; (h) an administrator under a deed of company arrangement executed by the entity; (i) a liquidator of the entity appointed in a voluntary winding up.

residential land means land in Australia if: (i) there is at least one dwelling on the land; or (ii) the number of dwellings that could reasonably be built on the land is less than the number prescribed by the regulations; and does not include land: (i) used wholly and exclusively for a primary production business; or (ii) on which the only dwellings are commercial residential premises.

relevant entity means any of the following: (a) an Australian entity; (b) a foreign corporation: (i) that holds relevant Australian assets; or (ii) that is a holding corporation of one or more Australian corporations; (c) a holding entity of such a foreign corporation or of an Australian unit trust.
subsidiary has the meaning given by subsections 21(1) to (3).

sections.source_quote: Section 4: Compilation notice imposing conditions means a written notice given under subsection 79H(1) and includes such a notice as varied under section 79J, 79P or 79Q.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential definitions for regulatory scope

sections.heading: Endnotes (cont.)
sections.title: Definitions of associates and independent directors for regulatory compliance
sections.summary: This section defines 'associate' and 'independent director', specifying who qualifies as an associate in the context of regulatory actions and who is considered an independent director for listed entities. It outlines the conditions under which individuals or entities are deemed associates, including relationships with substantial interests, senior officers, and trusts. The definitions are crucial for determining the scope of regulatory oversight and accountability.
sections.dtia_summary: These definitions clarify who qualifies as an 'associate' and 'independent director', which is critical for assessing the scope of data transfers and processing activities involving related entities. They help identify when entities are subject to joint regulatory oversight.

requirements.text: Each of the following persons is an associate of a person: (a) any relative of the person; (b) any person with whom the person is acting, or proposes to act, in concert in relation to an action to which this Act may apply; (c) any person with whom the person carries on a business in partnership; (d) any entity of which the person is a senior officer; (e) if the person is an entity: (i) any holding entity of the entity; or (ii) any senior officer of the entity; (f) any entity whose senior officers are accustomed or under an obligation (whether formal or informal) to act in accordance with the directions, instructions or wishes of: (i) the person; or (ii) if the person is an entity—the senior officers of the person; (g) an entity if the person is accustomed or under an obligation (whether formal or informal) to act in accordance with the directions, instructions or wishes of: (i) the entity; or (ii) the senior officers of the entity; (h) any corporation in which the person holds a substantial interest; (i) if the person is a corporation—a person who holds a substantial interest in the corporation; (j) the trustee of a trust in which the person holds a substantial interest; (k) if the person is the trustee of a trust—a person who holds a substantial interest in the trust; (l) if the person is a foreign government, a separate government entity or a foreign government investor in relation to a foreign country (or a part of a foreign country): (i) any other person that is a foreign government in relation to that country (or any part of that country); (ii) any other person that is a separate government entity in relation to that country (or any part of that country); or (iii) any other foreign government investor in relation to that country (or any part of that country).

A person is an independent director of an entity if: (i) a director of the entity; or (ii) for a unit trust—a director of a trustee of the entity; and (b) the entity is listed for quotation in the official list of a stock exchange (whether or not in Australia); and (c) the director meets the criteria of that stock exchange for a director to be independent (whether or not the criteria are binding).

sections.source_quote: Section 6: 6 Meaning of associate (1) Each of the following persons is an associate of a person: (a) any relative of the person; (b) any person with whom the person is acting, or proposes to act, in concert in relation to an action to which this Act may apply; ...
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential definitions for compliance scope

sections.heading: Endnotes (cont.)
sections.title: Definitions of business and interest types for regulatory purposes
sections.summary: This section defines when a foreign person starts an Australian business and specifies what constitutes an interest in securities, assets, trusts, or Australian land. It clarifies that starting a new business activity or acquiring interests in entities does not automatically constitute starting a business. The definitions also cover how interests in securities, assets, trusts, and land are determined, including joint holdings and remote interests.
sections.dtia_summary: These definitions clarify what constitutes an interest in assets or entities for regulatory purposes, which may impact how data transfers involving business entities are assessed under cross-border data transfer mechanisms.

requirements.text: A foreign person starts an Australian business if they start to carry on an Australian business or for a foreign person who already carries on an Australian business, the business starts a new activity that is not incidental to an existing activity and is within a different Division under the Australian and New Zealand Standard Industrial Classification Codes from the current activities.
A person holds or acquires an interest in a security if they have any legal or equitable interest in that security.
A person holds or acquires an interest in an asset if they have any legal or equitable interest in that asset.
A person holds or acquires an interest in a trust if they hold or acquire a beneficial interest in the income or property of the trust or an interest in a unit in a unit trust.
An interest in Australian land includes legal or equitable interests in Australian land, except for certain types of interests like leases, trusts, and agreements involving profits from land.

sections.source_quote: Section 8B(1) and (2): A foreign person starts an Australian business if they start to carry on an Australian business or for a foreign person who already carries on an Australian business, the business starts a new activity that is not incidental to an existing activity and is within a different Division under the Australian and New Zealand Standard Industrial Classification Codes from the current activities.
sections.relevance_reason: Definitions of business/interests for regulatory compliance

sections.heading: Endnotes (cont.)
sections.title: Definitions of business interests and percentage thresholds
sections.summary: This section defines what constitutes an interest of a specified percentage in a business, entity, or unincorporated limited partnership. It specifies that a person holds such an interest if their value of assets constitutes the specified percentage of the total assets of the business. The definitions also cover how voting power, distributions, and potential increases in interest percentages are determined for regulatory purposes.
sections.dtia_summary: These definitions clarify how interest percentages in entities are calculated, which may impact cross-border data transfers involving business entities and their data.

requirements.text: A person holds an interest of a specified percentage in a business if the value of the interests in assets held by the person, alone or together with one or more associates, is that specified percentage of the value of the total assets of the business.

A person holds an interest of a specified percentage in an entity if they are in a position to control at least that percentage of the voting power or potential voting power in the entity or hold interests in at least that percentage of the issued securities.

A person holds an interest of a specified percentage in an unincorporated limited partnership if they are in a position to control at least that percentage of the voting power or potential voting power in the partnership or are entitled to at least that percentage of the total distributions of capital, assets or profits.

sections.source_quote: Section 16A: A person holds an interest of a specified percentage in a business if the value of the interests in assets of the business held by the person, alone or together with one or more associates of the person, is that specified percentage of the value of the total assets of the business.
sections.relevance_reason: Defines financial data thresholds for regulatory control

sections.heading: Endnotes (cont.)
sections.title: Revocation of exemption certificates and action classification
sections.summary: This section specifies that the Treasurer must notify a person in writing of exemption certificate variations or revocations within 10 days. It clarifies that revocation or variation of an exemption certificate that no longer relates to an action does not prevent the action from being a notifiable action, a notifiable national security action, or a reviewable national security action. The time for the action to be classified is determined by the revocation or variation's effective date or the date of the revocation/variation. The section also states that the Treasurer may make disposal orders despite the action not being a core Part 3 action or contrary to national interest/security at the time of taking.
sections.dtia_summary: Revocation of exemption certificates affects classification of actions as notifiable or national security actions, requiring specific timeframes for classification and notification to ensure actions remain compliant with security and national interest thresholds.

requirements.text: Treasurer must notify person in writing of exemption certificate variation or revocation within 10 days
Exemption certificate variation or revocation does not prevent action from being a notifiable action
Exemption certificate variation or revocation does not prevent action from being a notifiable national security action
Exemption certificate variation or revocation does not prevent action from being a reviewable national security action
Time for action classification is the effective date of revocation/variation or the date of revocation/variation
Treasurer may make disposal orders despite action not being a core Part 3 action
Treasurer may make disposal orders despite action not being contrary to national interest or national security

sections.source_quote: Section 62B(5): The Treasurer must notify the person, in writing, of the variation or revocation before the end of 10 days after the revocation is made.
sections.relevance_reason: Treasurer's notification of exemption certificate changes

sections.heading: Endnotes (cont.)
sections.title: Prohibitions and disposal orders for foreign actions
sections.summary: This section outlines prohibitions on foreign persons taking certain actions, including acquisitions of agribusinesses, issuing securities, altering constituent documents, and entering significant agreements. It also details disposal orders that require foreign persons to dispose of interests within a specified period, with conditions to restore control or prevent changes in control. The Treasurer may make interim orders to restrict actions for up to 90 days and impose conditions on actions to ensure compliance with national security interests.
sections.dtia_summary: Prohibitions and disposal orders for foreign actions impact cross-border data transfers by restricting foreign entities from taking certain actions, requiring disposal orders to restore control, and imposing time limits on actions to ensure national security compliance.

requirements.text: Treasurer may prohibit foreign persons from acquiring agribusinesses
Treasurer may prohibit foreign persons from issuing securities in entities
Treasurer may prohibit foreign persons from altering constituent documents of entities
Treasurer may prohibit foreign persons from entering significant agreements
Treasurer may require foreign persons to dispose of interests within a specified period
Treasurer may make interim orders to restrict actions for up to 90 days
Treasurer may impose conditions to restore control of entities
Treasurer may require foreign persons to restore their position in entity management

sections.source_quote: Section 67: The Treasurer may, by notifiable instrument, make an order under (a) a significant action (other than a significant action covered by subparagraph (1A)(a)(ii)) is proposed to be taken; and (b) taking the significant action would be contrary to the national interest.
sections.relevance_reason: Treasurer's powers over foreign entities affect data security and cross-border transfers

sections.heading: Endnotes (cont.)
sections.title: No objection notifications for national security actions
sections.summary: This section outlines conditions for no objection notifications regarding actions that may affect national security. The Treasurer must ensure actions are not contrary to national security before granting no objection notifications. Notifications must specify actions, foreign persons, and include time limits for taking actions. The Treasurer may review actions under Division 3 if conditions are met.
sections.dtia_summary: No objection notifications for national security actions require the Treasurer to verify actions are not contrary to national security before approval. This affects cross-border data transfers involving national security risks.

requirements.text: Treasurer must ensure actions are not contrary to national security before granting no objection notifications
No objection notification must specify actions and foreign persons
No objection notification must include time limit for taking actions
Treasurer may review actions under Division 3 if conditions are met

sections.source_quote: Section 74(1A) and 75(1A) require the Treasurer to ensure actions are not contrary to national security before granting no objection notifications.
sections.relevance_reason: National security actions affect cross-border data transfers

sections.heading: Endnotes (cont.)
sections.relevance_reason: Endnotes are not relevant to compliance

sections.heading: Endnotes (cont.)
sections.title: National security review for actions with potential risks
sections.summary: This section requires the Treasurer to review actions that may pose national security risks before making orders. The review considers factors like false statements, material changes in business structure, or market conditions. The Treasurer must obtain national intelligence community advice and may notify the person involved, prohibiting the action until review completion unless specific conditions are met.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates a national security review for actions that could pose risks, requiring the Treasurer to assess whether an action creates a national security risk before imposing restrictions. It impacts DTIAs by introducing a review process for actions involving foreign entities.

requirements.text: Treasurer must review actions if specific conditions apply, including false statements or material changes
Treasurer must obtain national intelligence community advice on national security risks
Treasurer must notify the person in writing if reviewing an action, unless it prejudices national security
Treasurer may prohibit the action until review completion if no event occurs to end the prohibition
Treasurer may direct the person to take actions to address national security risks

sections.source_quote: The Treasurer may review an action if the Treasurer is satisfied that: (a) any of the following apply: (i) a no objection notification in relation to the action has been given and has not been revoked under section 76A; (ii) the action has been taken and, at the time it was taken, the action or the kind of action was specified in an exemption certificate in force under Division 5 of Part 2 or the regulations; etc.
sections.relevance_reason: Treasurer's national security review for financial actions

sections.heading: Endnotes (cont.)
sections.relevance_reason: Endnotes are not relevant to compliance

sections.heading: Endnotes (cont.)
sections.title: Treasurer's authority to vary notices imposing conditions
sections.summary: The Treasurer may vary notices imposing conditions on actions if satisfied the variation is reasonably necessary to eliminate or reduce national security risks. The variation must not disadvantage the person unless the Treasurer is satisfied it is necessary for national security. This applies to specific orders under sections 67, 68, and 69.
sections.dtia_summary: This section allows the Treasurer to adjust conditions on actions to address national security risks without disadvantaging individuals, relevant for assessing cross-border data transfers involving security-sensitive actions.

requirements.text: Treasurer may vary information specified under section 79H if satisfied variation is reasonably necessary for eliminating or reducing national security risk
Variation must not disadvantage the person unless Treasurer is satisfied it is necessary for national security

sections.source_quote: If a person was given a notice imposing conditions in relation to an action, the Treasurer may decide to vary the notice imposing conditions by: varying the information specified under section 79H; if the Treasurer is satisfied that the variation is reasonably necessary for purposes relating to eliminating or reducing the national security risk relating to the action.
sections.relevance_reason: National security focus, not data protection

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Foreign persons must notify Treasurer before taking notifiable actions
sections.summary: Foreign persons must notify the Treasurer before taking notifiable actions or notifiable national security actions. If section 18A applies, they must notify within 30 days of the action. The notice period is typically 40 days from receiving the notice or up to 90 days from registration of an interim order.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes notification requirements for foreign persons taking actions that could impact national security, relevant for assessing data transfers involving foreign entities.

requirements.text: Foreign person must give notice to Treasurer before taking notifiable action or notifiable national security action
If section 18A applies, foreign person must notify within 30 days after action

sections.source_quote: A foreign person must give the Treasurer a notice before taking a notifiable action or notifiable national security action or, if section 18A (increasing percentage of interests without acquiring additional interests in securities) applies, before the end of 30 days after the action is taken.
sections.relevance_reason: Foreign persons must notify Treasurer for data actions

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Foreign persons cannot take actions before notice period ends
sections.summary: Foreign persons cannot take actions during a specified period after giving notice to the Treasurer unless they receive a no objection notification or section 18A applies. The period is 40 days from receiving the notice or up to 90 days from registration of an interim order. The person may be prevented from taking the action by an order under Part 3.
sections.dtia_summary: This section restricts foreign persons from taking actions until the notice period ends, relevant for assessing timelines and compliance in cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: Foreign person must not take action before earliest of: 10 days after decision period ends, end of interim order period, or no objection notification

sections.source_quote: A foreign person must not take the action before the earliest of the following: the day that is 10 days after the end of the decision period mentioned in section 77; if an interim order is made—the end of the period specified in the order; the day a no objection notification is given to the person.
sections.relevance_reason: Irrelevant: tax administration, not data protection

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Failure to notify after no objection notification
sections.summary: A person who takes a core Part 3 action specified in a no objection notification must notify the Treasurer within 30 days of the latest of: the day they took the action, the day the no objection notification was given, or (for acquisitions) the acquisition date. Failure to notify incurs a civil penalty.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires timely notification after actions specified in no objection notifications, relevant for assessing compliance with data transfer conditions.

requirements.text: Person must notify Treasurer within 30 days of latest of: action date, no objection notification date, or acquisition date (for securities, assets, trusts, or land)

sections.source_quote: A person must give the Treasurer a notice in accordance with subsection (3) no later than 30 days after the latest of the following days: (a) the day on which the person took the core Part 3 action; (b) the day on which the no objection notice was given; (c) if the core Part  3 action is the acquisition of an interest in a security, asset, trust or Australian land, and section 15 applies in relation to the acquisition—the day on which the person acquired the interest in a security, asset, trust or Australian land (disregarding section 15).
sections.relevance_reason: Failure to notify after no objection notification

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Failure to notify after exemption certificate
sections.summary: A person who takes an exempt core Part 3 action specified in an exemption certificate must notify the Treasurer within 30 days of the latest of: the day they took the action, or (for acquisitions) the acquisition date. Failure to notify incurs a civil penalty.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires timely notification after actions specified in exemption certificates, relevant for assessing compliance with data transfer conditions.

requirements.text: Person must notify Treasurer within 30 days of latest of: action date, no objection notification date, or acquisition date (for securities, assets, trusts, or land)

sections.source_quote: A person must give the Treasurer a notice in accordance with subsection (3) no later than 30 days after the latest of the following days: (a) the day on which the person took the exempt core Part 3 action; (b) if the exempt core Part 3 action is the acquisition of an interest in a security, asset, trust or Australian land, and section 15 applies in relation to the acquisition—the day on which the person acquired the interest in a security, asset, trust or Australian land (disregarding section 15).
sections.relevance_reason: Failure to notify after exemption certificate

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Infringement notices for civil penalties
sections.summary: Provisions related to civil penalties are subject to infringement notices under the Regulatory Powers Act. This includes civil penalties for failing to notify the Treasurer, false or misleading information, and record-keeping requirements. These infringement notices apply to specific sections and subsections of the Act.
sections.dtia_summary: This section specifies that civil penalty provisions are subject to infringement notices, relevant for assessing enforcement mechanisms in data transfer compliance.

requirements.text: Provisions of Division 3 (civil penalties) are subject to infringement notices
Subsection 115D(1) (vacancy fee liability) is subject to infringement notices

sections.source_quote: The following provisions are subject to an infringement notice under Part 5 of the Regulatory Powers Act: (a) the provisions of Division 3 (civil penalties); (b) subsection 115D(1) (vacancy fee liability—vacancy fee return); (ba) subsection 115DA(1) (vacancy fee liability—false or misleading vacancy fee return); (c) subsection 115G(1) (vacancy fee liability—requirement to keep records); (d) section 130ZV (civil penalty for failing to give notice to Registrar).
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential: civil penalties not directly data protection

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Infringement notice penalties for regulatory violations
sections.summary: This section specifies penalty units for infringement notices under the Regulatory Powers Act. Tier 1 notices impose 12 units on individuals and 60 on corporations. Tier 2 notices impose 60 units on individuals and 300 on corporations. Tier 3 notices impose 300 units on individuals and 1,500 on corporations. The penalties apply to contraventions of civil penalty provisions listed in subsection (1).
sections.dtia_summary: Specifies penalty structures for regulatory violations that could impact cross-border data transfer compliance if penalties apply to data handling activities.

requirements.text: Tier 1 infringement notices impose 12 penalty units on individuals and 60 on corporations
Tier 2 infringement notices impose 60 penalty units on individuals and 300 on corporations
Tier 3 infringement notices impose 300 penalty units on individuals and 1,500 on corporations

sections.source_quote: The amount to be stated in an infringement notice for the purposes of paragraph 104(1)(f) of the Regulatory Powers Act for the alleged contravention of a civil penalty provision mentioned in subsection (1) of this section is: (a) for a tier 1 infringement notice (see subsection 101(1)): (i) given to an individual—12 penalty units; and (ii) given to a corporation—60 penalty units; and (b) for a tier 2 infringement notice (see subsection 101(2)): (i) given to an individual—60 penalty units; and (ii) given to a corporation—300 penalty units; and (c) for a tier 3 infringement notice (see subsection 101(4)): (i) given to an individual—300 penalty units; and (ii) given to a corporation—1,500 penalty units.
sections.relevance_reason: penalties for regulatory violations, not data compliance

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Issuing officer consent and nomination requirements
sections.summary: This section outlines conditions for a person to be an issuing officer under the Regulatory Powers Act. An issuing officer must be nominated in writing by the Minister and have consented to the role with active consent. The Secretary serves as the relevant chief executive for these provisions. Relevant courts include Division 2 of specified courts.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines who can act as an issuing officer for data-related actions, relevant for cross-border data transfers involving regulatory authorities.

requirements.text: The Minister must nominate the person in writing to be an issuing officer
The person must consent to be an issuing officer with active consent

sections.source_quote: (i) the Minister has, in writing, nominated the person to be such an issuing officer; and (ii) the person has consented to be such an issuing officer and the consent is in force.
sections.relevance_reason: irrelevant: nomination consent for regulatory officers

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Authorized officer and issuing officer definitions
sections.summary: This section defines authorized officers and issuing officers for evidential material under the Regulatory Powers Act. An authorized officer must be a magistrate, judge of a state or territory court, or judge of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2). The Secretary serves as the relevant chief executive for these provisions. Relevant courts include Division 2 of specified courts.
sections.dtia_summary: Specifies who can act as authorized officers for evidential material, relevant for cross-border data transfers involving regulatory authorities.

requirements.text: An authorized officer must be a magistrate
An authorized officer must be a judge of a state or territory court
An authorized officer must be a judge of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2) or Federal Court of Australia

sections.source_quote: a person is an issuing officer in relation to evidential material that relates to the provisions mentioned in subsection (1) if: (i) a magistrate; or (ii) a Judge of a court of a State or Territory; or (iii) a Judge of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2) or of the Federal Court of Australia
sections.relevance_reason: Pure definition of judicial roles, no data compliance link

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Fees for actions under foreign acquisitions regulations
sections.summary: This section outlines fees payable for actions related to foreign acquisitions and takeovers. Fees apply for applying for exemption certificates, giving notices of notifiable actions, significant actions, reviewable national security actions, and variations of no objection notifications. The Treasurer is not required to act before fees are paid, and fees may be waived or remitted.
sections.dtia_summary: Specifies fees for actions that could impact cross-border data transfers involving foreign entities and regulatory compliance.

requirements.text: Fees are payable for applying for an exemption certificate
Fees are payable for giving a notice of a notifiable action
Fees are payable for giving a notice relating to a significant action
Fees are payable for giving a notice relating to a reviewable national security action
Fees are payable for being given a notice under subsection 66A(4)
Fees are payable for applying for a variation of a no objection notification

sections.source_quote: Fees are payable for: (a) applying for an exemption certificate or a variation of an exemption certificate; and (b) giving a notice of a notifiable action or notifiable national security action; and (c) giving a notice relating to a significant action that is not a notifiable action or notifiable national security action; and (d) giving a notice relating to a reviewable national security action; and (e) being given a notice under subsection 66A(4); and (f) applying for a variation of a no objection notification or notice imposing conditions.
sections.relevance_reason: Fees for financial transactions unrelated to data compliance

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Vacancy fees for foreign residential land acquisitions
sections.summary: This section specifies vacancy fees payable by foreign persons for residential land where dwellings are occupied for less than 183 days in a year. The fee applies if the dwelling is genuinely occupied by the foreign person or their relative, under a lease/licence with 30+ days term, or available for occupation under such a lease/licence. The fee amount is set by regulations and may be waived or remitted. Foreign persons must submit vacancy fee returns to the Commissioner of Taxation annually and keep records for 5 years.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines vacancy fees for foreign residential land acquisitions that could impact cross-border data transfers involving property data.

requirements.text: A vacancy fee is payable by a foreign person for any dwelling on residential land if occupied for less than 183 days in a year
The dwelling must be genuinely occupied by the foreign person or their relative as a residence
The dwelling must be genuinely occupied under a lease or licence with 30+ days term
The dwelling must be genuinely available for occupation under a lease or licence with 30+ days term
The foreign person must submit a vacancy fee return to the Commissioner of Taxation after each vacancy year
The foreign person must keep all relevant records for 5 years after disposal of residential land interest

sections.source_quote: A vacancy fee is payable by a foreign person for any dwelling on residential land, for any year (called a vacancy year), if the dwelling is residentially occupied for less than 183 days in the year.
sections.relevance_reason: Tax administration, not data compliance

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Recovery of unpaid vacancy fees
sections.summary: This section states that unpaid vacancy fees for a dwelling may be recovered as a debt or by creating a charge over Australian land held by the foreign person. This charge also secures any unpaid penalties for contraventions of civil penalty provisions related to annual returns and record-keeping.
sections.dtia_summary: Specifies mechanisms for recovering unpaid vacancy fees, relevant for cross-border data transfers involving property data.

requirements.text: Unpaid vacancy fees may be recovered as a debt
Unpaid vacancy fees may be recovered by creating a charge over Australian land
The charge secures unpaid penalties for contraventions of civil penalty provisions

sections.source_quote: Unpaid vacancy fees for a dwelling may be recovered as a debt, or by the creation of a charge over Australian land in which an interest is held by the foreign person. The charge also secures any unpaid penalties for contraventions of civil penalty provisions under this Part relating to giving annual returns and keeping records.
sections.relevance_reason: Unrelated to data protection or cross-border transfers

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Vacancy fee payment due date and record retention
sections.summary: A person must pay a vacancy fee on a date specified in a notice at least 21 days after notice is given. The person must keep records of all transactions relevant to vacancy fee liability for each dwelling for at least 5 years after the end of each vacancy year. Records must be in English or readily convertible to English. The requirement does not apply if the Commissioner notifies the person they don't need to retain records or if the person is a finally dissolved company.

requirements.text: Vacancy fee payment due on a date specified in notice at least 21 days after notice is given
Keep records of transactions relevant to vacancy fee liability for each dwelling for each vacancy year
Retain records for at least 5 years after end of each vacancy year
Records must be in English or readily convertible to English
Do not retain records if Commissioner notifies person they don't need to retain records
Do not retain records if person is a finally dissolved company

sections.source_quote: A vacancy fee for a dwelling becomes due for payment on a day specified in the notice under section 115E, which must be at least 21 days after the notice is given to the person.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential record-keeping for financial data

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Vacancy fee penalties and recovery mechanisms
sections.summary: This section establishes a charge for unpaid vacancy penalties when a person fails to comply with vacancy fee returns, false returns, or record-keeping requirements. The charge is created when a declaration under section 115L comes into force. The section does not apply if the declaration under section 115L is not in effect at the time the charge is created.

requirements.text: Charge for unpaid vacancy penalties when person fails to comply with vacancy fee returns
Charge for unpaid vacancy penalties when person fails to comply with false or misleading returns
Charge for unpaid vacancy penalties when person fails to keep records
Charge created at time declaration under section 115L comes into force
Section does not apply if declaration under section 115L is not in effect

sections.source_quote: any amounts of pecuniary penalties (unpaid vacancy penalties) payable for contravention by the person of the following provisions, if the amounts have not been paid
sections.relevance_reason: Pure financial penalty mechanism, no data protection link

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Record-keeping for administrative actions
sections.summary: A person must keep records of actions specified in exemption certificates, compliance with conditions in no objection notifications, certain disposals of residential land interests, and register notices for 5 years (except for conditions requiring 2 years retention). Failure to maintain these records is an offence. Protected information obtained under this Act may only be disclosed for authorized purposes.

requirements.text: Make and keep records of actions specified in exemption certificates
Make and keep records of compliance with conditions in no objection notifications
Make and keep records of certain disposals of residential land interests
Make and keep records of register notices
Keep records for 5 years (except conditions requiring 2 years retention)
Protected information may only be disclosed for authorized purposes

sections.source_quote: A person must make and keep records relating to: (a) actions specified in exemption certificates; and (b) compliance with conditions in no objection notifications and exemption certificates; and (c) certain disposals of interests in residential land; and (d) register notices.
sections.relevance_reason: Record keeping with retention periods

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Records for national security actions
sections.summary: A person must maintain records of significant actions, notifiable actions, reviewable national security actions, exemption certificates, compliance with conditions, disposals of residential land interests, and register notices. Records must be in English or readily convertible to English and enable the relevant actions to be ascertained. The records must be retained for specific periods depending on the type of action.

requirements.text: Make and keep records of significant actions, notifiable actions, notifiable national security actions
Make and keep records of reviewable national security actions
Make and keep records of actions specified in exemption certificates
Make and keep records of compliance with conditions in no objection notifications
Make and keep records of compliance with conditions in notices imposing conditions
Make and keep records of disposals of residential land interests meeting specific criteria
Make and keep records of register notices
Records must be in English or readily convertible to English
Records must enable relevant actions to be readily ascertained

sections.source_quote: A person must make and keep records of every act, transaction, event or circumstance relating to the following: (a) any action taken by the person that is a significant action, notifiable action or notifiable national security action to the extent that the records are relevant to an order or decision under Part 3; (aa) any action taken by the person that is a reviewable national security action notified to the Treasurer to the extent that the records are relevant to an order or decision under Part 3;
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential record-keeping for specific national security actions

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Record retention periods for different actions
sections.summary: Records must be retained for specific periods: 5 years for significant actions, notifiable actions, notifiable national security actions, and disposals of residential land interests; 2 years for compliance with conditions; and 5 years for register notices. The records must be in English or readily convertible to English and enable the relevant actions to be ascertained.

requirements.text: Retain records for 5 years after action for significant actions, notifiable actions, notifiable national security actions
Retain records for 2 years after condition ceases to apply for compliance with conditions
Retain records for 5 years after disposal of residential land interest
Retain records for 5 years after register notice is given
Records must be in English or readily convertible to English
Records must enable relevant actions to be readily ascertained

sections.source_quote: A person must make and keep records of every act, transaction, event or circumstance relating to the following: (a) any action taken by the person that is a significant action, notifiable action or notifiable national security action to the extent that the records are relevant to an order or decision under Part 3; (aa) any action taken by the person that is a reviewable national security action notified to the Treasurer to the extent that the records are relevant to an order or decision under Part 3;
sections.relevance_reason: Record retention periods for financial data

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Protected information definition
sections.summary: Information obtained under, in accordance with, or for the purposes of this Act is protected information, except for information specified in exemption certificates or information obtained by certain public service employees under specific delegation or subdelegation arrangements. Protected information may only be disclosed for authorized purposes as defined in Division 355 of Schedule 1 to the Taxation Administration Act 1953.

requirements.text: Information obtained under, in accordance with, or for purposes of this Act is protected information
Protected information excludes information specified in exemption certificates
Protected information excludes information obtained by certain public service employees
Protected information may only be disclosed for authorized purposes

sections.source_quote: Information is protected information if the information is obtained under, in accordance with or for the purposes of this Act, except: (a) information specified in an exemption certificate given under: (i) section 57 (exemption certificates for new dwellings); or (ii) regulations made for the purposes of section 63; or (b) subject to subsection (2), information that is: (i) obtained by a person appointed to a position in the Australian Taxation Office, or a person engaged under the Public Service Act 1999 who is employed in the Australian Taxation Office; and (ii) obtained under, in accordance with or for the purposes of this Act as a result of a delegation or subdelegation under section 137, or a request under subsection 138(4).
sections.relevance_reason: Definitions of protected information

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Authorisation of disclosures of protected information for Commonwealth functions
sections.summary: A person may record, disclose, or use protected information if they perform functions under the Act or exercise powers. Disclosures must be to Commonwealth, State, Australian Capital Territory, Northern Territory officials, officers, employees, or persons appointed by the Commonwealth for this Act. The information must be used for the original purpose of disclosure.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines permissible disclosures of protected information to Commonwealth entities, relevant for cross-border data transfers involving government bodies.

requirements.text: A person may make a record of, disclose or otherwise use protected information if they perform functions or duties under this Act
Disclosures must be to a Minister, officer, or employee of Commonwealth, State, Australian Capital Territory, or Northern Territory
Disclosures must be to an officer or employee of a Commonwealth, State, or Territory body
Disclosures must be to a person appointed by the Commonwealth for the purposes of this Act
The information must be used for the purposes for which it was disclosed

sections.source_quote: 230 Compilation of the information that arise from the disclosure mentioned in paragraph (b) of this subsection.
sections.relevance_reason: Cross-border data transfer rules

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Authorisation of disclosures to Commonwealth Ministers and bodies for law enforcement
sections.summary: Permits disclosures of protected information to Commonwealth Ministers, their staff, or Commonwealth bodies for administering covered laws or discharging ministerial responsibilities. The relevant laws and matters are specified in subsections (3) and (5).
sections.dtia_summary: Specifies who can receive protected information disclosures for law enforcement purposes, relevant for cross-border data transfers involving government entities.

requirements.text: A person may disclose protected information to persons covered by subsection (2) for administering covered laws
A person may disclose protected information to persons covered by subsection (2) for discharging ministerial responsibilities
Persons covered include Ministers with responsibility for administering laws or matters
Persons covered include employees of Ministers under the Members of Parliament (Staff) Act 1984
Persons covered include officers or employees of Commonwealth departments administered by Ministers
Covered laws include Airports Act 1996, Australian Crime Commission Act 2002, and others
Covered matters include agriculture, water, infrastructure, telecommunications, industry policy, investment promotion, taxation policy, foreign investment, defence, and national security

sections.source_quote: 231 Compilation 122 Authorisation of disclosures to Commonwealth Ministers and Commonwealth bodies
sections.relevance_reason: Cross-border law enforcement data sharing

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Authorisation of disclosures to foreign governments and entities for national security
sections.summary: Permits disclosures of protected information to foreign governments or separate government entities if the Treasurer is satisfied there is a national security risk, the disclosure is not contrary to international law, and the information will be used per an agreement. The agreement must be between Commonwealth entities and foreign governments or entities.
sections.dtia_summary: Sets conditions for cross-border disclosures to foreign governments for national security, critical for DTIAs involving international data transfers.

requirements.text: A person may disclose protected information to a foreign government or separate government entity if they perform functions under this Act
A person may disclose protected information to a foreign government or separate government entity if they believe the disclosure will assist the foreign entity
The Treasurer must be satisfied that the information relates to a matter with a national security risk for Australia or the foreign country
The Treasurer must be satisfied that the disclosure is not contrary to international law
The person must be satisfied that the information will be used per an agreement
The foreign government or entity must undertake not to use or further disclose the information except per the agreement or as required by law
The agreement must be between Commonwealth entities and foreign governments or separate government entities

sections.source_quote: 234 Compilation 123B Authorisation of disclosures to foreign governments and separate government entities
sections.relevance_reason: Cross-border data transfer for national security

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Authorisation of disclosures for law enforcement activities
sections.summary: Permits disclosures of protected information to enforcement bodies if the person reasonably believes the disclosure is necessary for enforcement activities. The disclosure must be for law enforcement purposes under the Privacy Act 1988.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines when protected information can be disclosed to law enforcement bodies, relevant for cross-border data transfers involving law enforcement agencies.

requirements.text: A person may disclose protected information to an enforcement body if they reasonably believe the disclosure is necessary for enforcement activities
The disclosure must be for law enforcement activities under the Privacy Act 1988

sections.source_quote: 233 Compilation 123 Authorisation of disclosures relating to law enforcement
sections.relevance_reason: Law enforcement data sharing under Privacy Act

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Authorisation of disclosures to foreign governments for national security
sections.summary: Permits disclosures of protected information to foreign governments if the Treasurer is satisfied there is a national security risk, the disclosure is not contrary to international law, and the information will be used per an agreement. The agreement must be between Commonwealth entities and foreign governments or entities.
sections.dtia_summary: Sets conditions for cross-border disclosures to foreign governments for national security, critical for DTIAs involving international data transfers.

requirements.text: A person may disclose protected information to a foreign government or separate government entity if they perform functions under this Act
A person may disclose protected information to a foreign government or separate government entity if they believe the disclosure will assist the foreign entity
The Treasurer must be satisfied that the information relates to a matter with a national security risk for Australia or the foreign country
The Treasurer must be satisfied that the disclosure is not contrary to international law
The person must be satisfied that the information will be used per an agreement
The foreign government or entity must undertake not to use or further disclose the information except per the agreement or as required by law
The agreement must be between Commonwealth entities and foreign governments or separate government entities

sections.source_quote: 234 Compilation 123B Authorisation of disclosures to foreign governments and separate government entities
sections.relevance_reason: Cross-border data transfer for national security

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Authorisation of disclosures of non-identifying and public domain information
sections.summary: Permits disclosures of protected information that do not identify individuals or are already in the public domain, except where the disclosure contravenes this Division. Consents from individuals or related entities are also permitted for disclosures.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows disclosures of non-identifying or public domain information without additional restrictions, relevant for DTIAs involving anonymized or publicly available data.

requirements.text: A person may disclose protected information if it does not identify and is not reasonably capable of identifying a person
A person may disclose protected information if it is already in the public domain
A person may disclose protected information if the person to whom it relates gives written consent
A person may disclose protected information to the person or agent who provided it under this Act
A person may disclose protected information to an entity that is a member of the same wholly-owned group as the person who provided it

sections.source_quote: 235 Compilation 124 Authorisation of disclosures of non-identifying information
sections.relevance_reason: Permits disclosures of non-identifying/public data

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Authorisation for disclosure of protected information in legal proceedings
sections.summary: This section authorises disclosure of protected information to courts or tribunals for legal proceedings if the Commonwealth is a party and the Treasurer confirms it aligns with national interests. Disclosed information may only be used for the specific purpose of the disclosure. Individuals must not be compelled to provide protected information to courts or tribunals outside the scope of this Act.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires explicit authorisation for cross-border disclosure of sensitive information in legal proceedings, impacting data transfer to foreign courts.

requirements.text: A person who obtains protected information may disclose it to a court or tribunal for proceedings if Commonwealth is a party
Treasurer must be satisfied disclosure is not contrary to national interest
Disclosed information may only be used for the specific purpose of disclosure

sections.source_quote: A person who obtains protected information may disclose the information to a court or tribunal, or in accordance with an order of a court or tribunal, (whether within or outside Australia) for the purposes of proceedings if: (a) the Commonwealth is a party to the proceedings; and (b) the Treasurer is satisfied that it is not contrary to the national interest.
sections.relevance_reason: Cross-border data transfer authority for legal proceedings

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Good faith exception for protected information use
sections.summary: This section provides an exception to section 128 where protected information may be used in good faith when performing official duties or exercising powers under the Act. The defendant bears an evidential burden to prove good faith compliance.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows use of protected information across borders without penalty if used in good faith for official functions, relevant for cross-border data sharing.

requirements.text: Protected information may be used in good faith when performing or purportedly performing functions under the Act
Protected information may be used in good faith when exercising or purportedly exercising powers under the Act

sections.source_quote: Section 128 does not apply if the person makes a record of, discloses or otherwise uses protected information in good faith: (a) in performing, or purportedly performing, his or her functions or duties under this Act; or (b) in exercising, or purportedly exercising, his or her powers under this Act.
sections.relevance_reason: Good faith exception for protected information use

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Foreign ownership registration requirements
sections.summary: This section establishes that foreign persons acquiring or disposing of Australian assets must register with the Registrar. The Register records relevant actions and may require additional notices if circumstances change. No fee applies for registration, and civil penalties apply for non-compliance.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires foreign ownership data to be registered with Australian authorities, impacting cross-border asset transfers and data residency.

requirements.text: Foreign person must give register notice to Registrar when acquiring or disposing of Australian assets
Register may require further notices if asset interest changes or registered circumstance ceases
No fee payable for giving register notices

sections.source_quote: Generally, a foreign person who acquires or disposes of such an interest must give a register notice to the Registrar. The register notice may result in there being a registered circumstance in relation to the person, and the person may be required to give further register notices in relation to the registered circumstance.
sections.relevance_reason: Foreign ownership registration affects cross-border financial data transfers

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Register commencement and obligations
sections.summary: This section specifies that the Register of Foreign Ownership of Australian Assets must be maintained by the Registrar from the Register commencement day. It requires persons to give register notices to the Registrar for specific events, with some notices applicable to events occurring before the commencement date.
sections.dtia_summary: Establishes when foreign ownership data must be stored and registered, affecting data residency and cross-border transfer timelines.

requirements.text: Registrar must keep Register of Foreign Ownership of Australian Assets
Person must give register notice to Registrar on or after Register commencement day
Register notices may apply to events occurring before Register commencement day

sections.source_quote: The following apply on and after the day (the Register commencement day) that Part 2 of Schedule 3 to the Foreign Investment Reform (Protecting Australia's National Security) Act 2020 commences: (a) the Registrar's obligation under section 130S to keep the Register; (b) a requirement for a person to give a register notice to the Registrar.
sections.relevance_reason: Financial data cross-border transfer obligations

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Registrar's authority to add information to Register
sections.summary: The Registrar may add information to the Register from sources other than Division 3, including information obtained from registered circumstances or other relevant assets. Information obtained under Division 3 must be included in the Register as required by section 130T.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows Registrar to expand the Register with additional data sources, impacting data storage scope and cross-border data sharing.

requirements.text: Registrar may add information obtained under Division 3 to the Register
Registrar may add information related to registered circumstances
Registrar may add information obtained under Part 4 of Schedule 3

sections.source_quote: The Registrar may add to the Register any information obtained by the Registrar (other than information obtained under Division 3) relating to: (a) information obtained by the Registrar under Division 3; or (b) a registered circumstance (including a registered circumstance that has ceased); or (c) information obtained by the Registrar under Part 4 of Schedule 3 to the Foreign Investment Reform (Protecting Australia's National Security) Act 2020.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential governance for financial data

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Change in nature of interest in land
sections.summary: This section requires that if a foreign person's interest in land changes from one type to another, the Registrar must record the new registered circumstance. The foreign person must be aware or reasonably aware of the change to trigger this requirement.
sections.dtia_summary: Triggers updated registration when foreign ownership interests change, impacting cross-border data transfers and asset tracking.

requirements.text: Registered circumstance must exist in relation to person holding interest in land
Register must record new interest type when change occurs
Foreign person must be aware or reasonably aware of interest change

sections.source_quote: The Register records the registered circumstance as relating to the person holding any of the following kinds of interest: (i) an interest in residential land; (ii) an interest in commercial land; (iii) an interest in agricultural land; (iv) an interest in a mining or production tenement; (v) an interest (within the meaning of section 130ZB) in an exploration tenement.
sections.relevance_reason: Pure amendment mechanics

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Change in water resource share triggering registration
sections.summary: This section specifies conditions under which a registrable water interest change requires registration. It requires that the volume or share of a water resource changes, and the person holds the interest at the end of the financial year. The registrable event day is defined as the last day of the financial year.

requirements.text: The volume of water or share of a water resource changes
The person holds the registrable water interest at the end of the last day of the financial year

sections.source_quote: 251 Compilation (b) on that day or any of those days, the volume of water or the share of a water resource referred to in the registrable water interest changes; and (c) the person holds the registrable water interest at the end of the last day of the financial year.
sections.relevance_reason: Water resource share registration unrelated to data compliance

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Foreign person takes notifiable national security action
sections.summary: This section defines the conditions for when a foreign person's action constitutes a notifiable national security action. It requires that the foreign person takes an action that is a reviewable national security action under section 55D, and that they provided proposed actions to the Treasurer before taking the action.

requirements.text: The foreign person takes an action that is a reviewable national security action under section 55D
Before the action was taken, the foreign person gave a proposed action to the Treasurer

sections.source_quote: 261 Compilation the day on which the foreign person takes the notifiable national security action.
sections.relevance_reason: Defines cross-border security actions with Treasury oversight

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Foreign person takes reviewable national security action
sections.summary: This section outlines conditions for when a foreign person's action constitutes a reviewable national security action under section 55E. It requires that the foreign person takes an action that is a reviewable national security action under section 55E, and that they provided proposed actions to the Treasurer before taking the action.

requirements.text: The foreign person takes an action that is a reviewable national security action under section 55E
Before the action was taken, the foreign person gave a proposed action to the Treasurer

sections.source_quote: 264 Compilation 130ZM  Foreign person takes action that is a reviewable national security action under section 55E—businesses (a) the foreign person takes an action that is a reviewable national security action under section 55E, and: (ii) before the action was taken, the foreign person gave a proposed to be taken; or
sections.relevance_reason: Irrelevant: national security actions, not data transfers

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Change in interest in entity or business triggering registration
sections.summary: This section specifies conditions for when a change in interest in an entity or business requires registration. It requires that there is a registered circumstance in relation to the foreign person, the Register records the interest percentage, the percentage differs by 5% or more, and the foreign person is aware of the difference.

requirements.text: There is a registered circumstance in relation to the foreign person under a provision mentioned in subsection (4)
The Register records the foreign person as holding an interest in an entity or business of a particular percentage
The percentage interest differs from the percentage mentioned by 5% or more
The foreign person is aware, or ought reasonably to have become aware, of the difference

sections.source_quote: 266 Compilation (a) there is a registered circumstance in relation to the foreign person under a provision mentioned in subsection (4); and (b) in relation to that registered circumstance, the Register records the foreign person as holding an interest in an entity or business of a particular percentage; and (c) the percentage interest that the foreign person holds in the entity or business differs from the percentage mentioned in paragraph (b) by 5% or more; and (d) the foreign person is aware, or ought reasonably to have become aware, of the difference mentioned in paragraph (1)(c).
sections.relevance_reason: Registration triggers for foreign persons

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Registrar's annual report to Parliament
sections.summary: This section requires the Registrar to provide the Treasurer with an annual report detailing the operation of the Part during the financial year. The report must include statistics derived from the Register but must not identify or be capable of identifying a person. The report must be submitted by 30 June of the following financial year.

requirements.text: The Registrar must give the Treasurer a report for each financial year on the operation of this Part
The report must include statistics derived from information in the Register
The statistics must not identify or be capable of identifying a person
The Registrar must submit the report to the Treasurer by 30 June of the following financial year

sections.source_quote: 276 Compilation (1) The Registrar must give the Treasurer, for presentation to the Parliament, a report for each financial year that: (a) is on the operation of this Part during the financial year; and (b) includes statistics derived by the Registrar from information in the Register.
sections.relevance_reason: Annual report on data operations without identifying info

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Court orders for contraventions of foreign person rules
sections.summary: This section details court orders that may be made regarding contraventions of foreign person rules. It specifies conditions for when the court may order changes in control of Australian entities, acquisition of interests in securities, disposal of securities, or restrictions on senior officers.

requirements.text: The court may order whether the offender has been convicted of an offence
The court may order whether the contravention still continues
The court may order whether other proceedings relating to the contravention have been or are to be instituted
The court may order a change of control of an Australian entity or acquisition of interests in securities
The court may order the disposal of securities or assets
The court may order that any exercise of rights attached to securities be disregarded
The court may prohibit a person from acting as a senior officer of an entity

sections.source_quote: 279 Compilation (2) To avoid doubt, the court may make an order: (a) whether or not the offender has been convicted of an offence, or a civil penalty order has been made against the offender, in relation to the contravention; and (b) whether or not the contravention still continues; and (c) whether or not other proceedings relating to the contravention have been or are to be instituted.
sections.relevance_reason: court orders for foreign person rules

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Notice compliance exceptions for tax notices
sections.summary: This section allows tax notices or applications to be considered effective if the Secretary is satisfied they substantially comply with approved forms and it is appropriate to treat them as effective. It defines what constitutes a notice under the Act, including notices required by exemptions, conditions, and specific sections. It explicitly excludes register notices from this provision.
sections.dtia_summary: This section clarifies when tax notices are considered valid without full compliance, relevant for cross-border tax data transfers where notices are issued internationally.

requirements.text: Secretary must be satisfied notice substantially complies with approved form
Secretary must be satisfied it is appropriate to treat notice as effective
Notice includes notices required by exemption certificates
Notice includes notices required by conditions in notices imposing conditions
Notice includes notices under section 81
Notice includes notifications for paragraph 101(1)(b)
Notice includes notifications for subsections 98C(2), 98D(2) or 98E(3)
Notice includes notices mentioned in item 4 of table in subsection 113(1)
Section does not apply to register notices

sections.source_quote: Strict compliance with an approved manner or an approved form is required, unless the Secretary is satisfied in relation to a particular notice or application that: (a) the notice or application substantially complies with the approved manner or form; and (b) it is appropriate in the circumstances to treat the notice or application as being effective for the purposes of this Act.
sections.relevance_reason: Tax notices, financial data, no data protection relevance

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Taxation laws and enforcement provisions
sections.summary: This section clarifies that certain provisions of the Taxation Administration Act 1953 do not apply to tax matters under this Act. It also defines taxation officers under this Act and specifies that certain powers can be delegated to the Commissioner of Taxation.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant for data transfer impact assessments as it defines the scope of tax enforcement and delegation, affecting cross-border tax data processing.

requirements.text: Sections 255-10, Subdivision 255-D, Part 4-25 (except sections 288-35 and Division 298), Part 4-50, paragraph 355-15(c), Part 5-5, Division 444 do not apply
Secretary or public servant may be taxation officer under Taxation Administration Act 1953
Sections 353-10 and 353-15 may be delegated to Commissioner of Taxation

sections.source_quote: The following provisions of Schedule 1 to the Taxation Administration Act 1953 do not apply in relation to this Act: (a) section 255-10 (to defer the payment time); (b) Subdivision 255-D in that Schedule (security deposits); (c) Part 4-25 (charges and penalties), other than section  288-35 and Division 298 to the extent that it relates to that section; (d) Part 4-50 (release from particular liabilities); (e) paragraph 355-15(c) (application of Division 355); (f) Part 5-5 (rulings); (g) Division 444 (obligations of entities on behalf of other entities).
sections.relevance_reason: Taxation laws, no data compliance relevance

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: List of amending acts for tax legislation
sections.summary: This section provides a list of acts that amend tax legislation, including their numbers, years, assent dates, commencement dates, and application provisions. It details how these acts affect the tax administration framework.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant for data transfer impact assessments as it outlines legislative amendments affecting tax data processing across borders.

requirements.text: List of acts amending tax legislation with their details

sections.source_quote: Treasury Legislation Amendment (Application of Criminal Code) Act () 2001
sections.relevance_reason: Pure tax administration mechanics

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: List of consequential amendment acts for tax legislation
sections.summary: This section provides a list of acts that make consequential amendments to tax legislation, including their numbers, years, assent dates, commencement dates, and application provisions. It details how these acts affect the tax administration framework.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant for data transfer impact assessments as it outlines legislative amendments affecting tax data processing across borders.

requirements.text: List of acts making consequential amendments to tax legislation with their details

sections.source_quote: Australia's Foreign Relations (State and Territory Arrangements) (Consequential Amendments) Act 2020
sections.relevance_reason: Tax administration, not data compliance
